article.headline: Entity Structure & Accounting Decisions for Multi-Property Investors
article.alternativeHeadline: Optimize your real estate business entity structure and accounting practices for maximum tax efficiency
article.description: Complete guide to choosing the right entity structure (S-Corp, C-Corp, LLC, Partnership) and accounting methods for multi-property real estate portfolios.

image.description: Entity structure guide for real estate investors

article.keywords: business entity, LLC, S-Corp, C-Corp, tax planning, accounting methods, real estate accounting, multi-property portfolio

mainEntity.name: Should I use one LLC for all properties or separate LLCs for each property?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use separate LLCs if: you want asset protection (liability in one property doesn't affect others), you plan to sell individual properties (cleaner closing), or you have different partners for different properties. Use a single LLC if you prefer simplicity, lower annual fees, and unified accounting. Consider your state's LLC filing costs and complexity.

mainEntity.name: What's the difference between LLC taxation as a disregarded entity vs. S-Corp?

acceptedAnswer.text: Disregarded Entity (default): profits pass through to personal tax return, all net income subject to 15.3% self-employment tax. S-Corp Election: can pay yourself a 'reasonable salary' subject to payroll taxes, distribute remaining profits as dividends (no self-employment tax). S-Corp saves money if income exceeds ~$60k, but adds complexity and accounting costs.

mainEntity.name: When should I elect to be taxed as an S-Corp?

acceptedAnswer.text: Consider S-Corp election if: your real estate business generates net profit over $60,000 annually, you actively manage properties (not passive investor), and you're comfortable with additional accounting ($1,500-3,000/year). The self-employment tax savings typically pay for accounting costs once profit exceeds $75k.

mainEntity.name: How do I account for depreciation across multiple properties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depreciation is calculated per property: separate the building value from land value (typically 80/20 split), use a 27.5-year schedule for residential and 39-year for commercial. Aggregate all properties on Schedule E (Form 1040). Track cost basis and depreciation separately for each property to calculate gains correctly at sale.

mainEntity.name: What accounting method should I use—cash or accrual?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most small real estate investors use cash basis: simpler, matches when money is actually received/paid. Accrual basis required if you have more than $25M in gross receipts. Cash basis works fine unless you have complex financing or want GAAP-compliant statements for lending.

mainEntity.name: How should I handle related-party transactions between my entities?

acceptedAnswer.text: Document everything: interest rates must be arm's-length (comparable to third-party loans), management fees must be reasonable and actually paid, rental payments must match fair market value. IRS scrutinizes related-party transactions heavily. Work with a CPA to ensure transactions withstand audit.

mainEntity.name: What records should I maintain for each property?

acceptedAnswer.text: Maintain: original purchase documents showing cost basis, receipts for all repairs/improvements, tenant lease agreements and payment records, utility bills and insurance documents, property tax statements, mortgage statements, depreciation schedules, and bank statements. Keep these for 7 years minimum (IRS statute of limitations).

mainEntity.name: Should I use the passive activity loss rules to shelter other income?

acceptedAnswer.text: Only if you qualify as a real estate professional: >50% of service hours in real estate and material participation (100+ hours/year per property). If you qualify, you can deduct unlimited losses against other income. If not, you're limited to $25,000/year loss deduction plus any unused losses carried forward.
